skill.name: jira-workflow
skill.description: Orchestrate Jira story creation, approval, workflow transitions, and implementation updates using Jira REST API patterns.
skill.summary: Manage Jira stories from proposal through completion with approval-first workflow guidance.

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell, network, environment, sensitive-file, weak-crypto, and heuristic findings. Review found the shell and weak-crypto findings are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks, command examples, and Base64 Jira Basic auth, but the skill intentionally reads Jira credentials and performs authenticated Jira writes. No prompt injection or non-Jira credential exfiltration evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Jira Write Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill provides examples that create issues, create subtasks, transition issues, and add comments through Jira REST API calls. This is intended behavior, but it can modify project data when used with valid Jira credentials, so users should approve actions and scope tokens carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls and surrounding code clearly target Jira REST endpoints and include POST operations that change Jira state. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but the write impact is confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Environment Credentials Used for Jira API Authentication
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs use of JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_API_TOKEN, JIRA_BASE_URL, and JIRA_PROJECT_KEY, then builds a Basic Authorization header from those values. This is expected for Jira automation, but it exposes credential handling risk if logs, prompts, or configuration are mishandled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable and Authorization header usage is explicit in the skill. I found no evidence that credentials are sent outside the configured Jira base URL, so this is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Referenced Executable Scripts Are Not Included in Package
medium_findings.description: The skill describes ready-to-run Node.js and Python scripts and shows commands to execute them, but the scanned package contains only SKILL.md. Users cannot inspect those referenced scripts in this package, and execution guidance should be treated as incomplete unless the scripts are supplied and reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable script references are present, and the local file listing contains only SKILL.md. This creates a reviewability and user expectation risk, although no hidden script file is present in the package.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Triggered Shell Execution Heuristic
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby or shell backtick findings are false positives from Markdown inline code, code fences, JavaScript template literals, and example CLI commands. SKILL.md is documentation and does not contain an included executable script that automatically runs these snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are visibly Markdown lists, fenced examples, template strings, or command examples. The package file list shows no included executable code besides the Markdown skill file.

low_findings.title: Base64 Flag Is Basic Auth Encoding, Not Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings correspond to Base64 encoding used for a Jira Basic auth header and error-message strings that include HTTP status codes. Base64 is not being used as encryption, but API token handling should still be protected.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Base64 call is directly attached to the Jira Authorization header, which is the normal Basic auth format. The later status-code matches are not cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Public Documentation Links and Configured Jira URL
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are Jira and methodology references, plus an example Atlassian tenant URL. These are not suspicious destinations, but users should set JIRA_BASE_URL only to their trusted Jira tenant.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or placeholder configuration. I found no hardcoded webhook, paste site, or unrelated collection endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Requests with Authorization Header
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use fetch with an Authorization header for Jira issue creation, transitions, and comments. This pattern is sensitive because credentialed requests can mutate Jira data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code builds a Basic auth header and reuses it in Jira fetch calls. The destinations are configured Jira endpoints, so the pattern is sensitive but aligned with the stated purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Instructions in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes example node and python commands for workflow scripts. The referenced script files are absent from the scanned package, so users should not run external versions without separate review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in the documentation, but no corresponding scripts exist in the scanned package. This is a documentation and supply-chain caution rather than active code execution.

content.value_statement: Teams need Jira stories that match implementation work and move through the correct workflow states. This skill guides story proposals, approvals, Jira API updates, and completion comments.
actual_capabilities: Drafts SAFe-style Jira story proposals with acceptance criteria and definition of done.
Prompts for user approval before creating Jira issues.
Shows Jira REST API patterns for creating stories and subtasks.
Explains how to query and apply Jira workflow transitions.
Provides comment patterns for implementation details and work logs.
Maps Jira issue progress to Claude Code task tracking workflows.
limitations: Requires valid Jira email, API token, base URL, project key, and permissions.
Workflow states are tailored to one team-managed Jira setup and may need adjustment.
Referenced script files are not included in the scanned package.
Does not replace Jira permission checks, audit logs, or administrator controls.

use_cases.title: Create Approved Development Stories
use_cases.description: Turn feature requests into structured Jira stories with acceptance criteria and ask for approval before creation.

use_cases.title: Sync Implementation Progress
use_cases.description: Move Jira issues to Progressing or Done and add implementation notes after work is verified.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Sprint Workflow
use_cases.description: Review available transitions, keep issue status current, and connect task tracking with Jira updates.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Jira Story
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Jira story proposal for this request. Include persona, goal, benefit, acceptance criteria, complexity, and subtasks. Ask for approval before creating it.

prompt_templates.title: Create After Approval
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the approved story details to prepare a Jira issue creation plan. Confirm the project key, issue type, labels, and epic link before any API call.

prompt_templates.title: Transition Current Work
prompt_templates.prompt: For issue [ISSUE-KEY], query available transitions, choose the transition that matches [TARGET STATE], and explain the update before applying it.

prompt_templates.title: Complete and Document Work
prompt_templates.prompt: For issue [ISSUE-KEY], summarize modified files, commits, verification results, and notes. Add an implementation comment, then transition the issue to Done if approved.

output_examples.input: Turn the signup API task into a Jira story.
output_examples.output: A proposed story with summary, SAFe user story text, acceptance criteria, definition of done, complexity, subtasks, and an approval question.

output_examples.input: Move SCRUM-55 to Done and add implementation details.
output_examples.output: A transition plan that checks available Jira transitions, posts a concise implementation comment, and confirms completion status.

output_examples.input: Create subtasks for the approved authentication story.
output_examples.output: A subtask list grouped by implementation area, with a reminder to confirm parent issue and permissions before creation.

best_practices: Get explicit user approval before creating, transitioning, or commenting on Jira issues.
Use least-privilege Jira API tokens and store them only in the local environment.
Query available transitions before changing status because Jira workflows vary by project.
anti_patterns: Do not create or transition Jira issues from ambiguous requirements.
Do not paste Jira API tokens into prompts, comments, tickets, or logs.
Do not assume the documented workflow states match every Jira project.

faq.question: Does this skill create Jira issues automatically?
faq.answer: It instructs the assistant to ask for approval before creating issues. Users should keep that approval step enabled.

faq.question: What Jira credentials are needed?
faq.answer: It expects a Jira email, API token, base URL, project key, and optional board ID in environment variables.

faq.question: Can it work with company-managed Jira projects?
faq.answer: Yes, but epic linking and workflow fields may differ. The skill notes different handling for team-managed and company-managed projects.

faq.question: Does the package include runnable scripts?
faq.answer: No. The scanned package contains SKILL.md only, even though the documentation references script names.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses Jira credentials and authenticated network writes. That is intended, but it can change real project data.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
